core_behavior.note: Model B defines special character as any non-letter, non-digit character (broader), while Models A and C restrict to specific printable ASCII sets, and models differ on whether all rules must be simultaneously reported vs. short-circuit.

inputs.note: Model A explicitly addresses null/undefined/non-string inputs and whitespace handling; Models B and C only describe a string input with minimal detail, omitting null and non-string type handling; none address Unicode normalization or non-string type distinction explicitly in inputs; critical behaviors around null, empty string, and non-string type are absent from B and C.

outputs.note: Model A returns a structured object with a boolean and an errors array with specific messages; Model B returns a boolean optionally with error messages; Model C returns only a boolean with no error detail.

assumptions.note: Model A restricts uppercase/lowercase/digit to ASCII ranges only and excludes space from special characters; Model B uses Unicode for all character classes and includes a broader special character set; Model C assumes no non-printable characters and treats space as invalid; these are explicitly incompatible on Unicode handling and whitespace-as-special-character.

failure_modes.note: Model A returns a distinct error response for non-string inputs; Model B returns false with an error message for null/empty; Model C returns false for null, empty string, or non-string; none explicitly address not logging the password value on rejection.

edge_cases.note: Model A treats whitespace as a valid character counting toward length but not as a special character; Model B treats whitespace as valid if criteria are met; Model C treats spaces and non-printable characters as invalid, returning false.

security_constraints.note: Model A has no security constraints; Model B specifies plaintext storage prohibition, secure hashing, and brute-force limits; Model C specifies no logging/storing of passwords and injection resistance; the critical behavior of not logging the password on rejection is absent from Model A.

performance_constraints.note: Model A specifies no performance constraints; Models B and C both specify sub-100ms response time and throughput requirements, with Model C adding a 1000 requests/second threshold.

summary: CON and ASM each appeared most frequently, tied at 2 occurrences each across the eight fields.
